inputs.label: Equivalent Weekly Rent (A$)
inputs.hint: Comparable rental in your target area

howToSteps.name: Enter Property and Rent Details
howToSteps.text: Enter the purchase price of your target property and the equivalent weekly rent for a comparable property in the same area. Both inputs drive the true cost comparison.

howToSteps.name: Set Mortgage Rate and Deposit
howToSteps.text: Enter your expected mortgage interest rate and deposit percentage. A 20% deposit avoids LMI and reduces monthly repayments.

howToSteps.name: Choose Your Timeframe
howToSteps.text: Select how many years you plan to compare. Buying typically becomes more advantageous over longer periods due to capital growth and equity build-up, while renting wins in the short term due to lower upfront costs.

howToSteps.name: Review the Trade-Off
howToSteps.text: The calculator shows total cost of buying vs renting over the period, equity built through ownership, and the net financial advantage of buying (accounting for opportunity cost of deposit).

faq.q: Is it better to rent or buy in Australia right now?
faq.a: It depends on your city, timeframe, and financial situation. In 2026, with home loan rates at approximately 6.3%, buying is typically more expensive month-to-month than renting in most capital cities. However, if you plan to stay for 7–10+ years and the market appreciates at historical rates (~4–5% p.a.), buying generally builds more long-term wealth than renting and investing the difference.

faq.q: What upfront costs do I need to budget when buying in Australia?
faq.a: Typical upfront costs include stamp duty (the largest cost — see our First Home Buyer Calculator for state-specific figures), building and pest inspection ($400–$800), legal/conveyancing fees ($1,500–$3,000), loan application fees ($0–$600), LMI if LVR >80%, and moving costs. In NSW, buying an $800,000 property incurs stamp duty of approximately $31,490 for an established property (non-first home buyer).

faq.q: What ongoing costs does home ownership involve beyond the mortgage?
faq.a: Home owners must budget for council rates ($1,200–$2,500/year), water rates ($800–$1,500/year), home and contents insurance ($1,500–$3,000/year), strata fees if applicable ($2,000–$10,000+/year), maintenance (typically 1–2% of property value annually), and land tax for investment properties.

faq.q: Does the rent money dead money argument hold up?
faq.a: Not entirely. While home ownership builds equity, renters avoid opportunity costs such as stamp duty, maintenance, and capital tied up in a deposit. If a renter invests their deposit and the difference between rent and mortgage repayments in diversified assets, they can build comparable wealth — though most Australians find the enforced saving of mortgage repayments more effective in practice.

faq.q: How does negative gearing affect the rent vs buy decision for investors?
faq.a: Negative gearing allows investors to deduct rental property losses (where interest and expenses exceed rental income) against other income, reducing their tax bill. This can make the true after-tax cost of holding an investment property lower than the gross figures suggest. However, it does not eliminate the risk of capital loss or a prolonged period of negative cash flow.

examples.heading: Sydney — $800,000 home, 10-year comparison
examples.text: Buying an $800,000 property in Sydney with a 20% deposit at 6.3% over 30 years costs approximately $3,962/month in mortgage repayments. Renting a comparable property at $650/week costs $2,817/month. Over 10 years, buying costs approximately $594,000 (including interest, rates, and maintenance) while renting costs $338,000. However, the buyer builds approximately $320,000 in equity and benefits from expected capital growth of approximately $400,000, making buying advantageous after ~7 years.

examples.heading: Regional area — $450,000 property, 5-year comparison
examples.text: In a regional city where a $450,000 property can be rented for $400/week, buying with a 20% deposit ($90,000) at 6.3% means monthly repayments of approximately $2,228 vs rent of $1,733. Over 5 years, buying is more expensive in cash terms, but builds approximately $80,000 in equity. Whether buying wins depends heavily on local capital growth.

schema.appDescription: Compare the true financial cost of renting versus buying a home in Australia, including stamp duty, mortgage interest, capital growth, and equity build-up
